**1 replication attempt of this paper has been recorded in the FORRT Library of Reproduction and Replication Attempts (FLoRA).**



> Meyers-Manor et al. (2020). A shoulder to cry on: Heart rate variability and empathetic behavioral responses to crying and laughing in dogs. Canadian Journal of Experimental Psychology / Revue Canadienne de Psychologie Exp-rimentale, 74(3), 235-243. https://doi.org/10.1037/cep0000225
> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** "successful"
> 
> _From the quote source:_ Like Custance and Mayer, dogs showed more behaviours directed toward the person crying, whether the owner or the stranger, than during baseline or laughing conditions.
> **Quote source:** "abstract"


Manual inspection may help to assess what led to the outcome described. Future research can learn from replications and build on them. More information about why and how we submitted this automated comment was submitted can be found [here](https://github.com/forrtproject/flora-pubpeer/blob/main/README.md)

> _From the quote source:_ My key test was the odds ratio associated with the amount that subsidies change the choice towards a child care type, the first row in model 1, in the confirmatory analysis section above. In the authors original model, they found an odds ratio of 1.025, meaning that a unit increase in subsidies for the corresponding childcare alternative is associated with a 2.5% increase in the odds of preferring that mode of childcare. My experimental data produced a fairly similar result in terms of the key test. My computed odds ratio was 1.037, which is close in magnitude and the same level of significance, meaning the impact of subsidy on parent child care choice in my experiment was quite similar to the original. || My experimental data produced a fairly similar result in terms of the key test. > R-seler et al. (2021). Evidence against subliminal anchoring: Two close, highly powered, preregistered, and failed replication attempts. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 92, 104066. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2020.104066
> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** "failed"
> 
> _From the quote source:_ To sum up, due to the extremely high power, the anchoring effect should have occurred if it indeed existed. We interpret the fact that there was no sign of anchoring-even after we excluded outliers and controlled for whether participants saw the flickering-as inconsistent with the claim that subliminal anchoring exists.
> **Quote source:** "pg.3"
